data.title: Pattern Group: Revenue Growth Remains Strong, And The Moat Got Better
data.description:
Summary

I gave a buy rating to Pattern Group (PTRN) in my initiation post, with my key thesis being that PTRN has a solid data moat that should get better as it scales up, and that moat de-risks the growth path ahead. I am even more convinced that PTRN is a buy after looking at recent developments. Revenue growth was strong, newer marketplaces are scaling, and the platform moat looks stronger.

The moat is getting harder to copy

Previously, the main argument for PTRN's moat was that EXP was a strong analytics and optimization tool and that it could help users to optimize the "Traffic x Conversion x Price x Availability" equation. And I think PTRN's moat has gotten a lot better since then. Per management disclosure, the Pattern intelligence layer is now powered by more than 66 trillion data points (vs. 47 trillion six months ago), and more importantly, it was noted that the automation engine executed 5.53 billion marketplace bid changes and 40 million price changes in 2025. These are insanely huge numbers that tell us adoption is real. There are actual entities leveraging PTRN's tool for live pricing and ad decisions, and it is collecting all the data to improve itself.

Analytics and optimization are great, but it is how PTRN helps to execute this operationally that makes it even more interesting. PTRN now has 22 global offices and supports >70 marketplaces, and its products can now reach marketplaces in ~1.5 days. More importantly, days inventory outstanding [DIO] improved to 72 days, down 10 days y/y. My takeaway is that PTRN is able to leverage those 66 trillion data points to further de-risk this inventory-bearing business model, and the numbers speak for themselves. There appears to be no sign of PTRN's pace of scaling slowing either. If PTRN manages to hit 100 trillion data points (should be achieved by this year), can DIO drop further to 60 days? That is possible, and if we extrapolate this, that changes the moat angle a bit. The moat is not just PTRN having better software plus scale anymore. Its moat gradually becomes more like a connected system of data, automation, logistics, and speed. From this angle, competitors would need more than just a dashboard that shows all the analytics and numbers. They would need the same trillions of data points, the marketplace connections, and the physical infrastructure.

New marketplaces are scaling faster

I am also bullish, as PTRN's marketplace rate of scaling up is getting faster and looking more durable. In the call, management said non-Amazon revenue grew 94% y/y in Q4, and international revenue grew 69% y/y. It also said Coupang, TikTok Shop, and Walmart saw triple-digit y/y growth in the quarter. While we don't know the absolute figures, the rate of growth is huge and tells me that these newer channels are contributing real growth. Another data point that convinced me that demand is real is that more than two-thirds of brands now work with PTRN across multiple marketplaces, which also tells you this is becoming part of the operating model rather than a side effort. This matters as it should create a flywheel. As more brands use PTRN across more marketplaces, PTRN gets more experience/data points, which helps in sharpening execution. That should make it easier to bring on board the next brand onto those same marketplaces and easier to move existing brands into even more channels. In other words, each successful rollout is not just driving revenue growth; it is also enhancing PTRN's ability to repeat this.

And I think we can see this "learning experience" showing up in the speed of onboarding. Management said PTRN added 12 marketplaces in FY2025 and also said marketplace number two took a couple of years to build, while newer marketplaces can now be added much faster because the platform has become more modular. One example is Coupang, which went from zero in 2024 to $11 million in 2025. The faster PTRN can get these channels onboard and running, the faster it is able to scale up, which accelerates the flywheel effect and also diversification (which makes the growth more durable).

Valuation

PTRN grew even faster than I thought it could. My initial assumption was FY2025 to see 30% y/y revenue growth, and PTRN beat that by a full 900 bps. That said, I am keeping my forward growth assumptions the same at 30% (rolling 1 year forward to FY2028). Note that management guidance for FY26 revenue growth of ~26% is compared against a high 2025 comp of 39% y/y growth. The momentum is real, and given the enhanced moat and flywheel effect, I stay convinced PTRN can continue to grow rapidly. By my math, compounding PTRN's revenue by ~high-20% from FY26 through FY28 would result in total revenue of ~$5.3 billion in FY28.

For valuation, PTRN's forward revenue multiple has contracted to ~0.5x as I write this update, which I don't think is fair given the growth profile relative to peers. As I mentioned before, peers like HubSpot (HUBS), Salesforce (CRM), EverCommerce (EVCM), and Braze (BRZE) have a much slower revenue growth profile (high single digits to low 20%) vs. PTRN. EVCM is literally trading at ~4x forward revenue with high single-digit revenue growth, for reference. PTRN should easily trade much higher. I am sticking to just 1x for my model, and that is enough to give me an attractive return profile.

Investment Risk

As the world moves towards using AI to help in shopping, it could hurt PTRN's moat. Say that more discovery and checkout move toward AI shopping agents, social commerce, or other channels that are outside traditional third-party marketplaces; PTRN may need to spend more to defend its position (like figuring out how to engage with AI agents, etc.). That would mean more margin pressure that delays the timeline for PTRN to print meaningful profits.

Conclusion

I remain bullish on PTRN because my thesis is playing out. PTRN is still putting up strong growth, the moat looks better, and newer marketplaces are scaling faster. I am expecting PTRN to continue printing this solid growth, and multiples should re-rate upwards in time.

data.title: Dole: Still Expensive, But I'm Raising My Price Target
data.description:
One of the advantages of living a bit everywhere in the world, with extended stays in tropical places, is the access to farm-fresh fruit of the exotic variety. For the past 2 years, I have been eating more mango, pineapple, papaya, passion fruit, jackfruit, and various other fruits that in most areas in the Western World only exist as what I would consider pale imitations (unripe, different in taste, smaller) or preserved.

Now, in order to get the taste when I am in northern climes, I'm left with the canned/preserved variety. Because I like these fruits so much now, I'm left in turn with the best quality of such options.

That was actually one of the reasons I started covering Dole PLC (DOLE) about a year ago, in an article you can find here.

Since that time, Dole has exhibited a fair bit of volatility but has overall performed as I expected it to. It has not generated alpha to its investors (lest you did targeted buying at lower prices more in line with my original PT), and its current RoR to my article sits at about negative 1%, slightly positive if you were to include dividends.

Dole PLC is essentially a play on fruit and, in some cases, vegetables. Since 2021, its formation by the combination of Total and Dole, it has remained one of the largest companies that handles the products of bananas and pineapples, but also more easily obtained produce such as berries and salads. Its market-leading position in both NA and Europe for key tropical fruits that have become staples to consumers makes for an interesting investment opportunity at the right price. Dole is not a company that grocery stores or major grocers can easily go without. It controls the critical infrastructure from the point of farming to the shelf, and the scale, where it actually operates its own shipping services (10+ vessels) and distribution facilities means that it can compete at a level that is nigh impossible for retailers to facilitate themselves, especially the retailers that themselves are geographically far away from areas where growing such produce is even possible.

Even if there are competitors, the scale (again) would make it very hard for these competitors to offer produce at the same price, quality, or shipping because the more intermediaries you include in your process, usually the more the price goes up.

This is what makes Dole attractive, but we also need a good price, and we'll need to look into how the company has been doing as of late.

Dole PLC - Performance in 2025 Leading to 2026E

So, the advantages from an operational perspective of Dole are very clear. Its scale and operations can ensure a year-round supply of what are essentially farm-fresh (with processing, admittedly) staple fruits like pineapples and bananas. Controlling the supply chain means a reduction of costs, which means both higher profit for Dole and higher potential for lower sales prices for end users - a win-win. Furthermore, Dole is typically a trusted brand.

If we look at fundamental issues with its balance sheets or finances, we find a few noteworthy ones. One of them is leverage.

Net debt is still about $1B as of late 2025, as you can see above, though down from about $1.48B in 2021. Why would that be a problem for a business that generates exceeding $9B worth of revenues following the merger?

Because, dear readers, these companies' costs are very high. Conversely, its profits are what I would consider below grocery level. Despite improvements since the merger, when Dole PLC had a margin of only 150 bps (operating level, not net margin, nor EBIT margin (EBIT is non-GAAP), it's still, as of 2025 at below 2.5%. So when you come to ~$215M worth of OI, and when over $65M of that money goes to interest expense, a more than doubling since the period of 2021 (and when another $20M goes to FX), your net income is going to be low. Ergo, the room for interest rate hikes or coverage on interest is much lower than you might expect for a business like this. Much of the debt down payment has been due to divestments, not the company moving net income to pay down - given the relatively low levels of that income.

Net interest for the company was at an all-time low (excepting the negative margins in 2021) and came to 0.6%.

In a sense, the way I choose to interpret this data is that scale has not helped Dole increase its net margins. The company may be, dear readers, a very solid operator with a good supply chain and assets, but it's certainly not all that profitable. Its gross margins are below 10%, and that's below even most grocers.

The issue seems to be that much of the revenue is absorbed by the company's extensive operations. And contrary to what one might expect, the company's pricing power is actually lower than what one might expect, given its commodity-like nature.

Furthermore, Dole has actually struggled with produce (vegetables), a segment that was actually net-negative in terms of profit. This has thankfully been on a turnaround stretch. FX, as you could see from my description above, is also a contributing factor to this. What the company could do fundamentally to combat this is to remove or pay down debt. Given, however, its dividend, which eats up about $34.2M based on about 95M (multiplied by $0.34-$0.36) shares outstanding, and how the other distribution of net income and EBIT looks, the company is very limited in addressing its net debt. At current income levels, even saying that it would take 10 years to pay off is an optimistic estimate, unless the company were to significantly improve its efficiency or profit. I view this as unlikely.

The company refers to its EBITDA for results. However, this excludes cash required for the business by treating recurring expenses as one-time expenses (this is not something that just Dole does; many companies do this). Because of this, I will be focusing strictly on GAAP metrics, such as operating and net income.

I therefore consider, with conviction, the 2025 results reported some time back to be relatively weak. Fruit costs, SCM pressures, and divestiture impacts pressured the company. Dole can do little about rising costs and inflation - these are external factors, but that does not change the fact that they are pressuring the business. Climate risks, such as tropical storms (Sara had a big impact on Dole, apparently). The fact that the progressive produce segment, divested in 2024, has not been replaced has also led to a lower overall revenue and earnings potential.

Meanwhile, while costs and inflation have gone up, Dole has not been able to price products at a higher level. This convergence of costs and margin pressure resulted in a decrease in profitability. Its core segments, pineapples and bananas, were good, but Dole is struggling when it comes to other products, such as cherries, grapes, and others, where the company does not have as big a moat as in the others.

Do not misunderstand me here in believing I am saying that Dole is a bad business to invest in. However, given razor-thin margins and more risks to those margins than I see upside, this demands significant attention to valuation and price prior to investing.

As of 2026, my view is the following.

Dole Valuation - potentially higher in 2026, but not by much

In my last piece, I gave Dole a conservative PT of $12/share. This amounts, for the 2026E period, to about a P/E of 8x, compared to the current P/E of 11.48x. I may have been a bit low there. As of this article, I am raising my PT for the company to $13/share.

My rationale for this increase is as follows.

Dole has been trading at a deep discount compared to its peers. Because recent trends include the company divesting non-core assets, focusing on higher margins, as well as the repayment of debt, this slight increase signifies consideration of that fact.

$13/share comes to a double-digit current P/E of about 10x, without, for that matter, going too high. It puts investors at a P/B of 1x (compared to below 1x), while still deeply discounting its sales due to the margin issues (we're talking around 0.15x to sales, at most 0.2x). It takes into account the company's turnaround potential, but at the same time doesn't put investors in a position where the turnaround being forecasted has to happen. Rather, even in a position where the company continues to trade cheaply due to climate effects or poor results, you're still holding Dole PLC at what I would consider to be a good value.

If I were to describe what a PT of $13/share represents in words, I would say that Dole PLC, at that price, is a "slow-growth grower and trader of fruits" rather than a high-margin multi-field/product player. I believe Dole PLC wants to be viewed as a highly efficient logistical platform, given how it operates. The company may have the potential to get there, but it is not there right now.

In a world where debt is lower or gone, the company could easily reach margins and efficiency, putting it above 5% compared to below 3% today. At that level, I would certainly allow a 13-15x P/E. The reasons that fruit and snack peers, which usually trade at 10x to EBITDA, trade so much higher than Dole PLC (which trades at about 5.5x if we put it at $13/share) and why this is a justified discount, in my view, are weather disruptions, shipping risks, and logistical hurdles, which result in thin profitability aside from its current debt profile.

I believe $13/share more fairly accounts for the company's eventual upside, where I could see a 12-13x P/E, but I believe that buying here would be valuing the company a bit too high.

Risks for the company are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

I view companies through two lenses - historical valuation and peer valuation—and then consider special factors for the company and how it may grow going forward. When it comes to Dole, I like the company, for sure. I just don't like where I am being asked to buy it (in terms of price), given what else is available. The reason for this is related to fundamentals and operational risks, which I consider to be a non-negotiable factor at this point.

Thesis

Dole PLC is a well-known, international brand supplying tropical fruits and produce to geographies around the world. Its main products are fruits/products that cannot grow anywhere but in tropical climates (barring, of course, massive, efficient farming operations with climate control). The company has gone through several changes in the last few years and is now lower-leveraged and with good upside and stability at a conservative investment entry point. However, given some of the risks presented, I believe that the entry point should not be here - even if it's close to that.

I give the company a conservative 9-11x P/E range price, coming to about $13/share to get the upside that I "want" from the company. Because of this, Dole plc is a "hold" to me as of March 2025.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this and a price target of $13/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of March 2026.

data.title: Karyopharm: Putting The Phase 3 Trial In Context
data.description:
Topline Summary and Update

I've followed Karyopharm Therapeutics (KPTI) for quite a while now with optimism that has not matched reality, placing "Buy" sentiments on the company in 2024 and then later that same year, only to see the valuation of the stock decline by more than 50% as the market has awaited the results of a key, arguably existential phase 3 trial. We now have a first look at the results of that trial, and the market has not been pleased, sending the share price from a high of $8.90 to the near-$5 price point that it sits in at the time of writing. Today, I want to take another look at the company, with the intention of outlining why I think there is reason for optimism here.

Pipeline Updates

Selinexor

For years now, KPTI has been working ardently toward finding new niches for their XPO1 inhibitor selinexor outside of the multiple myeloma space. A readout for endometrial cancer remains a bit on the horizon, with the latest guidance from management suggesting that results should be coming in mid-2026. This relates to the phase 3 XPORT-EC-042 trial evaluating selinexor as a maintenance therapy for TP53 wildtype endometrial cancer; recall that this trial builds on a signal from the SIENDO study, which met its PFS endpoint but was not considered clinically relevant, but a signal of major benefit was identified in the TP53-wildtype subgroup of patients.

So we will wait for the results of XPORT-EC-042 with bated breath. The news we have today relates to a topline readout of the phase 3 SENTRY trial, which compared the addition of selinexor to ruxolitinib versus ruxolitinib alone for patients with treatment-naive myelofibrosis.

The trial met only one of its two primary endpoints. Adding selinexor improved the rate of patients achieving a 35% reduction in spleen size (rates of 50% and 28% for combination and ruxolitinib alone, respectively), but the co-primary endpoint of total symptom score was not improved (9.89 vs 10.86 point improvement).

This actually echoes the findings from MANIFEST-2, a phase 3 study evaluating the addition of the BET inhibitor pelabresib to ruxolitinib in a similar group of patients. Pelabresib plus ruxolitnib had a spleen reduction rate of 65.9% vs 35.2% for ruxolitinib alone, but total symptom score was not different. Notably, pelabresib has not been pushed forward to getting FDA approval at this time, and Novartis is spinning up another phase 3 trial to try pelabresib in patients with higher-risk disease.

For the SENTRY trial, KPTI has seen an interesting signal of improved overall survival with the use of selinexor, with 4.7% vs 10.2% of patients having died in the experimental and control arms, respectively (stratified hazard ratio, 0.43; one-sided nominal P value 0.0222). Median overall survival was 11.56 and 12.58 months, respectively, reflecting how immature these data are.

Reduction of the JAK2 variant allele fraction (a marker of how persistent the disease is) was also observed more frequently with selinexor (32% vs 24%).

Tolerabiliy, as has long been the case with selinexor, was a snag for the study. Nausea rates were increased from 17.2% to 57.3%, and the rate of discontinuing treatment due to AEs increased from 8.6% to 14.5%. Neutropenia and thrombocytopenia rates were also elevated with selinexor therapy.

As part of the presentation, KPTI brought in MPN expert John Mascarenhas to discuss the results, who noted that the overall survival signal was encouraging, and in line with known predictors of overall survival (ie, higher spleen volume response rate, reduction in JAK2 allele burden). He ended with the following:

I believe the combination should be available to all patients, although this may not be the preferred treatment selection for every patient. I look forward to presenting the greater data set from the pivotal trial at upcoming meetings.

That summarizes what is a decided mixed bag of results that KPTI is moving forward with bringing to the FDA for potential approval consideration, per their presentation of the results. A final timeline for their plan toward a supplemental NDA is anticipated within the next 6 months.

Financial Overview

As of the end of 2025, KPTI held $60.5 million in cash and equivalents, along with $3.2 million in investments. They had a senior secured term loan of $21.0 million, as well as various convertible senior notes totaling $111 million. Total revenue for the year was $146.1 million, more or less the same as it was in 2024 and 2023. Operating expenses were $236.8 million, and after interest and extinguishment of debt, the annual net loss was $196 million.

The net cash used for operations was $75.4 million. At this burn rate, KPTI has under one year of funds to continue. This was before the proceeds of an anticipated private placement with RA capital that will bring in $30 million in exchange for shares and warrants.

Strengths and Risks

Mixed - A path to approval, but not one that's certain

I have to be honest: this phase 3 trial is a tough one. Missing one of the primary endpoints is not a good sign for ultimate approval. And that has played out pretty poorly for pelabresib, now finding itself tied to more years of study before it could potentially be added to the mix.

Selinexor appears to be in a similar boat, winning on spleen volume but missing on symptoms. And add the toxicity to boot, and the deck looks stacked against a possible approval.

But this phase 3 trial isn't "tough" because I'm disappointed in the results. It's tough because they have a signal of overall survival improvement that looks encouraging. Even after 96 weeks follow-up for MANIFEST-2, overall survival looked almost completely the same down the line. SENTRY appears to have something to it, and I could potentially see a conditional approval coming on the basis of the positive primary endpoint and this overall survival signal.

However, that's very much not clear to happen at this point, and that does not bode well for the near-term prospects of the investment thesis.

Risk - Dire cash situation even after their private placement

Even with over $100 million in net product revenues, KPTI is facing a cash crisis, and their own guidance during the last corporate update put their runway only "into Q2 2026." Certainly, the $30 million private placement is a shot in the arm, but KPTI has not definitively solved their cash issue with this move, and even under the best circumstances with the FDA, they are looking at a potential approval not coming sooner than late in 2026, and that would be quite an optimistic timeline. How exactly they will continue to fund operations beyond 2026 has not yet been determined, and that makes for a highly risky situation for the investment thesis.

Bottom-Line Summary

KPTI is entering yet another period of uncertainty following the readout of a key phase 3 trial. Will SENTRY yield a new drug approval for selinexor? It's not possible to tell at this point. It's definitely in play, but it's not great to feel this amount of uncertainty about the prospects for this agent.

Couple that with the rather short cash runway that KPTI holds, and it's very easy to see why the market has reacted with substantial pessimism about the value of this stock. And that, honestly, presents the bulk of the opportunity here. KPTI is beaten down, but they now have multiple potential catalysts on deck for 2026 that could present a reversal of fortunes. We will see an initial readout of the endometrial cancer study. We'll get more information about the myelofibrosis study. Positive interactions with the FDA around myelofibrosis, or a positive readout for endometrial cancer (which would, recall, just be a confirmation of the signal from the subgroup analysis of SIENDO) would very likely bring a substantial positive re-evaluation from the market.

And for that reason, I continue to see the near-term upside potential as being worthy of a tentative "Buy" sentiment. That being said, you should not read that as an "unequivocal" recommendation. KPTI is entering into territory that poses existential risks. They have less than a year of cash on hand, and that is not enough time to get to approval. It is likely that they will need to raise funds to continue operations, and there is no guarantee they'll be able to do that.

Therefore, although I am reiterating my "Buy" sentiment at these levels, I would absolutely caution against going too deep into it. And it should be pointed out that the average volume of the stock is relatively low at around 1.2 million shares, which could impede one's ability to enter or exit a position.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
